nodes.title: Right-of-Use Assets and Lease Liabilities
nodes.summary:
# Description of Partial Document

This document section covers the company's accounting policies and financial details related to property, equipment, and leases:

## Main Points:

1. **Depreciation and Capitalization Policies**: Details on depreciation timelines for various asset categories (3-10 years for fixtures, equipment, and software), treatment of repair and maintenance costs as expenses, and capitalization of major renewals and internally developed software.

2. **Property and Equipment Summary**: Comprehensive breakdown of property and equipment balances as of December 31, 2022 and 2021, including:
   - Land, buildings, fixtures, leasehold improvements, and software
   - Total gross property and equipment of $34.6 billion (2022)
   - Accumulated depreciation of $21.5 billion
   - Net property and equipment of $13.1 billion

3. **Depreciation Expense Trends**: Annual depreciation expense increased from $2.1 billion (2020) to $2.4 billion (2022), including amortization of finance/capital leases.

4. **Impairment Charges**: Reference to $261 million impairment recorded in 2021 related to planned retail store closures.

5. **Right-of-Use Assets and Lease Liabilities**: Accounting treatment of operating leases under current standards, including:
   - Recognition methodology based on present value of future lease payments
   - Treatment of renewal options (generally not included unless reasonably certain)
   - Handling of variable lease costs and short-term leases
   - Distinction between lease and nonlease components

6. **Goodwill**: Brief introduction to business combination accounting using the acquisition method.

nodes.title: Goodwill
nodes.summary:
# Main Points Covered in the Partial Document

## Property and Equipment
- Depreciation policies for various asset categories (buildings: 20-40 years; fixtures/equipment/software: 3-10 years)
- Repair and maintenance costs are expensed; major renewals are capitalized
- Application development costs for internally developed software are capitalized and depreciated
- Property and equipment summary for 2022 and 2021, showing total net value of $13.1 billion and $12.9 billion respectively
- Depreciation expense totaled $2.4 billion in 2022, with a $261 million impairment charge in 2021 related to retail store closures

## Right-of-Use Assets and Lease Liabilities
- Accounting treatment for lease arrangements under current lease accounting standards
- Recognition of right-of-use assets and lease liabilities at lease commencement based on present value of future minimum lease payments
- Treatment of renewal options (generally not included in lease term unless reasonably certain)
- Short-term leases (≤12 months) are not recorded on balance sheets
- Variable lease costs (sales-based payments, taxes, maintenance, insurance) are expensed as incurred

## Goodwill and Intangible Assets
- Goodwill accounting under the acquisition method; not amortized but subject to annual impairment testing
- Identifiable intangible assets include trademarks, trade names, customer contracts, technology, and provider networks
- Definite-lived intangibles are amortized using straight-line method; indefinite-lived intangibles are tested annually for impairment
- 2021 goodwill impairment charge of $431 million related to the LTC reporting unit

## Asset Impairment Testing
- Long-lived asset impairment evaluation procedures using undiscounted and discounted cash flow analyses
- 2022 office real estate optimization charges of $117 million related to flexible work arrangements
- 2021 store impairment charge of $1.4 billion related to planned closure of approximately 900 retail stores
- Goodwill impairment testing using discounted cash flow and market multiple methods

nodes.title: Intangible Assets
nodes.summary:
# Main Points Covered in the Partial Document

## Intangible Assets and Goodwill Management
- The company's identifiable intangible assets include trademarks, trade names, customer contracts/relationships, covenants not to compete, technology, provider networks, and VOBA (value of business acquired)
- Goodwill is not amortized but subject to annual impairment reviews
- Definite-lived intangible assets are amortized using straight-line method (except VOBA, which is amortized based on contract life)
- Indefinite-lived intangible assets are not amortized but tested annually for impairment

## Recoverability of Long-Lived Assets
- The company evaluates long-lived assets for impairment when events or circumstances indicate carrying value may not be recoverable
- Impairment testing involves comparing carrying amounts to undiscounted and discounted future cash flows
- Impairment losses are recorded when carrying value exceeds estimated future cash flows

## Specific Impairment Charges
- 2022: $117 million office real estate optimization charges related to corporate office space reduction and flexible work arrangements
- 2021: $1.4 billion store impairment charge related to planned closure of approximately 900 retail stores; $431 million goodwill impairment charge on the LTC reporting unit
- 2020: No material impairment charges on long-lived assets

## Goodwill and Indefinite-Lived Intangible Assets Testing
- Goodwill impairment testing uses discounted cash flow and market multiple methods
- Indefinite-lived intangible assets (trademarks) tested using relief from royalty method
- No goodwill impairments recorded in 2022 and 2020

nodes.title: Recoverability of Long-Lived Assets
nodes.summary:
# Main Points Covered in the Partial Document

## Intangible Assets and Goodwill
- Goodwill is not amortized but subject to annual impairment reviews
- Identifiable intangible assets include trademarks, trade names, customer contracts, covenants not to compete, technology, provider networks, and VOBA
- Definite-lived intangible assets are amortized using straight-line method (except VOBA, which is amortized based on contract life)
- Indefinite-lived intangible assets are tested annually for impairment

## Recoverability of Long-Lived Assets
- Company evaluates recoverability when events indicate carrying value may not be recoverable
- Impairment testing uses undiscounted cash flows first, then discounted cash flows if needed
- 2022: $117 million office real estate optimization charges related to flexible work arrangements
- 2021: $1.4 billion store impairment charge for planned closure of approximately 900 retail stores
- 2020: No material impairment charges

## Goodwill Impairment Testing
- Fair value estimated using discounted cash flow and market multiple methods
- 2022 and 2020: No goodwill impairments identified
- 2021: $431 million goodwill impairment charge on LTC reporting unit

## Indefinite-Lived Intangible Assets
- Tested by comparing fair value to carrying value using relief from royalty method
- No impairment losses recognized in 2022, 2021, or 2020

## Separate Accounts and Health Care Costs Payable
- Separate Accounts assets carried at fair value for large case pension products
- Health care costs payable includes unpaid claims, IBNR estimates, and capitation costs
- IBNR estimates developed using actuarial principles, historical claim patterns, and health care cost trends
- Completion factors used to estimate ultimate claim costs based on historical payment patterns

nodes.title: Separate Accounts
nodes.summary:
# Description of Partial Document

This document section covers three main accounting topics:

1. **Impairment Testing of Indefinite-Lived Intangible Assets**: Describes the income approach methodology for assessing asset impairment, noting that no impairment losses were recognized on indefinite-lived intangible assets during 2020-2022.

2. **Separate Accounts**: Explains the accounting treatment of separate accounts related to large case pension products, where contract holders bear investment risk. These assets are carried at fair value, legally segregated from the company's other businesses, and investment income accrues directly to contract holders without being reflected in consolidated financial statements.

3. **Health Care Costs Payable**: Details the estimation and accounting of health care liabilities, including:
   - Components of payable amounts (unpaid claims, capitation costs, risk-sharing arrangements, state assessments)
   - Incurred But Not Reported (IBNR) claims estimation methodology using actuarial principles
   - Critical assumptions including historical claim payment patterns and health care cost trend rates
   - Completion factors methodology for estimating ultimate claim costs based on the time between service dates and payment dates
   - Special considerations for claims incurred within three months of the financial statement date

nodes.title: Health Care Costs Payable
nodes.summary:
# Main Points Covered in the Partial Document

## Asset Impairment and Valuation
- Discussion of impairment loss recognition when carrying value of assets exceeds estimated fair value
- No impairment losses were recognized on indefinite-lived intangible assets in 2020-2022

## Separate Accounts
- Assets and liabilities related to large case pension products carried at fair value
- Contract holders bear investment risk
- Assets are legally segregated and not subject to claims from other company businesses
- Management fees included in services revenue

## Health Care Costs Payable
- Comprises unpaid medical, dental, pharmacy claims, capitation costs, and risk-sharing arrangements
- Includes IBNR (Incurred But Not Reported) estimates for services rendered but not yet reported or paid
- Estimates developed using actuarial principles considering historical claim patterns, medical cost trends, utilization, and membership changes
- Critical assumptions include historical/projected claim payment patterns and health care cost trend rates
- Completion factors used to estimate ultimate claim costs, with different methodologies for claims incurred within vs. beyond three months
- Health care cost trend rates affected by utilization changes, unit costs, product design, provider contracts, and demographic factors
- Considerable variability and uncertainty inherent in estimates, particularly for recent claims
- Quarterly re-examination of previously established estimates based on actual claim payments

## Other Insurance Liabilities - Unpaid Claims
- Reserves for short-duration group disability and term life insurance contracts
- Based on present value of future benefits using assumed investment yields and mortality/morbidity assumptions
- IBNR estimates developed using actuarial principles and completion factors
- Certain liabilities discounted based on expected investment returns
- Subject to change due to experience, investment yield, and other factors
- Specific balances reported for December 31, 2022 and 2021

nodes.title: Other Insurance Liabilities
nodes.summary:
# Main Points Covered in the Partial Document

## Health Care Costs Payable
- The Company uses actuarial judgment to estimate health care cost trend rates, emphasizing recent claim incurred dates to account for seasonal patterns and membership changes
- Multiple factors influence health care cost trends including per member utilization, unit costs, product design, provider contracts, medical management, and demographic characteristics
- The estimation process involves considerable variability and uncertainty, particularly for claims incurred within three months of the financial statement date
- The Company re-examines estimates quarterly based on actual claim payments and changes in facts and circumstances
- Estimates may develop favorably or unfavorably and could be material if health care cost trend rates or completion factors change

## Other Insurance Liabilities - Unpaid Claims
- Unpaid claims reserves are maintained for short-duration group disability and term life insurance contracts, including IBNR estimates
- Reserves are based on present value of future benefits using assumptions regarding mortality, morbidity, and recoveries
- IBNR estimates for life products use completion factors based on historical experience
- As of December 31, 2022, unpaid claims balances totaled $243 million (current) and $1.1 billion (long-term)
- Life and disability insurance liabilities have been substantially ceded to third parties through reinsurance, though the Company remains obligated to policyholders

## Future Policy Benefits
- Reserves for limited payment pension and annuity contracts use actuarial principles with mortality, retirement, expense, and interest rate assumptions
- Assumed interest rates ranged from 3.0% to 11.3% for pension and annuity contracts
- Long-term care contract reserves represent present value of future benefits less future premiums, with assumed interest rates of 4.9% (2022) and 5.1% (2021)
- As of December 31, 2022, future policy benefits totaled $385 million (current) and $5.0 billion (long-term)

## Premium Deficiency Reserves
- The Company evaluates contracts to determine if losses are probable when expected future claims exceed existing reserves plus anticipated premiums
- No premium deficiency reserves existed as of December 31, 2022; a $16 million reserve was established in 2021 for Medicaid products

## Policyholders' Funds
- Reserves equal cumulative deposits less withdrawals plus interest credited, net of experience-rated adjustments
- Interest rates for pension and annuity investment contracts ranged from 3.5% to 4.8% in 2022 and 2021
- HSA funds of $2.9 billion held at December 31, 2021 were eliminated following the PayFlex sale in June 2022

## Self-Insurance Liabilities
- The Company is self-insured for general liability, workers' compensation, auto liability, and certain health and medical liabilities
- Self-insurance accruals use standard actuarial assumptions and historical claims experience
- Self-insurance liabilities totaled $1.1 billion at both December 31, 2022 and 2021

nodes.title: Foreign Currency Translation and Transactions
nodes.summary:
# Description of Partial Document

This document section covers accounting policies for foreign currency translation and revenue recognition:

## Foreign Currency Translation and Transactions
- Explains treatment of non-U.S. dollar functional currency locations, where assets/liabilities are translated at end-of-period rates, revenues/expenses at average rates, and equity at historical rates
- Describes U.S. dollar functional currency locations with remeasurement of foreign currency items at end-of-period or historical rates depending on account type
- Notes that foreign currency gains/losses were immaterial for 2020-2022

## Revenue Recognition - Health Care Benefits Segment
- Details that revenue derives primarily from insurance premiums and fees billed to customers
- Explains Commercial business revenue based on contracted rates per member and covered member counts
- Describes Government business revenue collected monthly from federal government and agencies based on fixed rates and member eligibility
- Addresses retroactivity adjustments for enrollment changes and the company's estimation process

## Premium Revenue
- Premiums recognized in the month enrollees are entitled to services
- Reported net of allowances for terminations, uncollectible amounts, and estimated ACA minimum medical loss ratio (MLR) rebates
- Unearned premiums recorded as insurance liabilities until earned
- Describes treatment of experience-based premium adjustments

## Services Revenue
- Covers administrative services contracts including claim processing and member services
- Explains ASC fee revenue recognition over service period
- Details performance guarantees and financial risk provisions with revenue offsets

## Medicare Part D Accounting
- Briefly introduces PDP insurance premiums based on annual bids and CMS contractual arrangements

nodes.title: Revenue Recognition
nodes.summary:
# Description of Partial Document

This document section covers two main accounting topics for a healthcare and pharmacy company:

## Foreign Currency Translation and Transactions
- Explains accounting treatment for non-U.S. dollar and U.S. dollar functional currency locations
- Details how assets, liabilities, revenues, and expenses are translated or remeasured at different exchange rates
- Notes that foreign currency gains/losses were immaterial for 2020-2022

## Revenue Recognition Policies (Primary Focus)
Detailed revenue recognition methods across four business segments:

**Health Care Benefits Segment:**
- Premium revenue from insurance billings recognized monthly when enrollees are entitled to services
- Adjustments for retroactivity, medical loss ratio rebates, and unearned premiums
- Services revenue from administrative services contracts with guarantees
- Medicare Part D revenue including risk corridor adjustments and subsidies

**Pharmacy Services Segment:**
- Revenue from mail service and retail pharmacy networks using gross method when company controls drugs
- Recognition at delivery (mail) or point-of-sale (retail)
- Adjustments for manufacturer rebates and pricing guarantees

**Retail/LTC Segment:**
- Retail pharmacy revenue at point of customer possession
- Loyalty program accounting (ExtraCare, CarePass)
- Long-term care revenue with adjustments for reimbursement differences
- Walk-in medical clinic revenue for completed services

The document emphasizes variable consideration estimates, performance obligations, and adjustments for discounts, rebates, and guarantees across all segments.

# Description of Partial Document

This document excerpt covers key accounting and operational definitions for a pharmacy and retail company:

## Main Points:

1. **Pharmacy Services Network Definitions**
   - Pharmacy network includes retail and specialty retail pharmacies, excluding Maintenance Choice activity
   - Mail choice includes mail order facilities and specialty claims, plus Maintenance Choice prescriptions
   - Maintenance Choice allows eligible members to fill prescriptions via mail or CVS retail at the same price

2. **Contract Liabilities and Customer Receivables**
   - Contract liabilities primarily represent obligations to provide goods/services for received consideration
   - Include ExtraBucks Rewards and unredeemed gift cards
   - As of December 31, 2022: Trade receivables of $8,983 million and contract liabilities of $71 million
   - Contract liabilities decreased from $87 million (2021) to $71 million (2022) due to redemptions exceeding new issuances

3. **Cost of Products Sold Accounting**
   - **Pharmacy Services**: Includes prescription drug costs, shipping, handling, and operating costs of mail and retail pharmacies
   - **Retail/LTC Segment**: Includes merchandise costs, purchasing, warehousing, delivery, and inventory losses
   - Both segments account for costs net of volume-related discounts

4. **Vendor Allowances and Purchase Discounts**
   - The document begins explaining how the Pharmacy Services segment accounts for vendor allowances and purchase discounts from manufacturers, wholesalers, and retail pharmacies

nodes.title: Vendor Allowances and Purchase Discounts
nodes.summary:
# Description of Partial Document

This document excerpt covers several key accounting and operational topics:

## Pharmacy Services Definitions
- Defines the pharmacy network as claims filled at retail and specialty pharmacies, excluding Maintenance Choice activity
- Defines mail choice as claims filled at mail order facilities and specialty mail claims, including Maintenance Choice prescriptions

## Contract Balances and Customer Receivables
- Explains that contract liabilities represent obligations to provide goods/services for received consideration, primarily ExtraBucks Rewards and unredeemed gift cards
- Provides financial data showing trade receivables of $8,983 million (2022) and $7,932 million (2021)
- Details contract liability changes, including rewards earnings, gift card issuances, redemptions, and breakage

## Cost of Products Sold Accounting
- Describes how the Pharmacy Services segment accounts for costs, including prescription drug costs, shipping, and operating expenses
- Explains how the Retail/LTC segment accounts for merchandise costs, warehousing, and inventory losses

## Vendor Allowances and Purchase Discounts
- Details how the Pharmacy Services segment receives and recognizes purchase discounts from manufacturers, wholesalers, and retailers
- Explains how the Retail/LTC segment accounts for vendor allowances, including treatment of promotional reimbursements and upfront payments

## Health Care Reform Provisions
- Discusses the Health Insurer Fee (HIF) imposed under the ACA, which was repealed for years after 2020
- Explains the permanent risk adjustment program for transferring funds between insurance plans
- Details the temporary risk corridor program, including the Company's successful $313 million lawsuit recovery in 2020

nodes.title: Health Care Reform
nodes.summary:
# Main Points Covered in Partial Document

## Purchase Discounts and Rebates (Pharmacy Services Segment)
- Three forms of purchase discounts: direct discounts at purchase, prompt payment discounts, and post-dispensing rebates from manufacturers
- Rebates recognized when prescriptions are dispensed and reconciled within 30 days of quarter-end
- Historical reconciliation adjustments have been immaterial
- Additional volume-based discounts available under wholesaler contracts
- Administrative service fees received from pharmaceutical manufacturers
- All discounts and fees recorded as reduction of cost of products sold

## Vendor Allowances (Retail/LTC Segment)
- Vendor allowances reduce inventory carrying costs and are recognized in cost of products sold when inventory is sold
- Allowances for promotional programs/services treated as reimbursements
- Advertising-linked allowances reduce advertising expense when commitment is satisfied
- Upfront vendor payments linked to purchase commitments are deferred and amortized over contract life based on sales volume
- Upfront payments not linked to purchase commitments are deferred and amortized on straight-line basis
- Amortization amounts have been immaterial

## Health Care Reform Provisions

### Health Insurer Fee (HIF)
- Annual premium-based fee imposed by ACA beginning January 1, 2014
- Repealed for calendar years after 2020
- Company recorded $1.0 billion HIF expense in 2020; no expense in 2021-2022

### Risk Adjustment Program
- Permanent ACA program transferring funds between plans based on risk scores
- Company estimates receivable or payable based on member risk relative to market average

### Risk Corridor Program
- Temporary ACA program expired end of 2016
- Company recovered $313 million owed in October 2020 following Supreme Court ruling
- Recovery recorded as premium revenue increase in 2020, resulting in $307 million pre-tax and $223 million after-tax income

nodes.title: Advertising Costs
nodes.summary:
# Main Points Covered in the Partial Document

This document excerpt outlines key accounting policies and practices:

1. **Advertising Costs** - Net advertising costs (after vendor funding) are expensed when incurred and included in operating expenses, with amounts of $745 million (2022), $707 million (2021), and $613 million (2020).

2. **Stock-Based Compensation** - Measured at grant date fair value and recognized as expense over the requisite service period (typically 3-5 years) using the straight-line method.

3. **Income Taxes** - The company uses the asset and liability method for tax accounting, recognizes deferred tax assets and liabilities, establishes valuation allowances when appropriate, and records uncertain tax positions using a two-step process based on technical merits and probability of realization.

4. **Defined Benefit Pension and Other Postretirement Employee Benefit Plans** - The company sponsors frozen pension and OPEB plans, recognizes their funded status on the balance sheet, and includes non-service cost components in other income.

5. **Earnings Per Share** - Calculated using the treasury stock method based on weighted average common shares outstanding.

6. **Shares Held in Trust** - The company maintains grantor trusts holding approximately one million shares designated for employee compensation plans, which are excluded from shares outstanding calculations.

nodes.title: Variable Interest Entities
nodes.summary:
# Description of Partial Document

This document section covers the Company's accounting treatment and disclosure of Variable Interest Entities (VIEs), which include:

## Primary Beneficiary - Red Oak Sourcing
- A 50/50 joint venture with Cardinal Health established in 2014 for generic pharmaceutical sourcing
- The arrangement was extended in 2021 from 10 to 15 years total
- The Company is deemed the primary beneficiary because it has power to direct Red Oak's activities
- Red Oak is consolidated in the Company's financial statements within the Retail/LTC segment
- Cardinal makes quarterly milestone-based payments to the Company (totaling $183 million annually in 2020-2022) that reduce inventory carrying value

## Other Variable Interest Holders
The Company invests in VIEs where it is not the primary beneficiary:
- **Hedge fund and private equity investments**: Generate investment returns for insurance business portfolios
- **Real estate partnerships**: Primarily low-income housing developments where benefits derive from tax credits and tax benefits

The Company records these non-primary beneficiary VIEs as long-term investments and recognizes its proportionate share of income/losses. Maximum loss exposure is limited to investment balances, which totaled $1.537 billion at December 31, 2022 (comprised of $589 million in hedge funds, $707 million in private equity, and $241 million in real estate partnerships).

The section also briefly mentions a related party equity method investment in SureScripts, a clinical health information network, with annual usage fees of approximately $52-60 million.

nodes.title: Related Party Transactions
nodes.summary:
# Description of Partial Document

This document section covers the following main points:

1. **Variable Interest Entities (VIEs)** - The Company's investments in three types of VIEs: generic pharmaceutical sourcing entities, hedge fund and private equity investments, and real estate partnerships, with explanation of how the Company evaluates primary beneficiary status.

2. **Red Oak Sourcing, LLC** - A 50/50 joint venture with Cardinal Health established in 2014 for generic pharmaceutical sourcing, where the Company is the primary beneficiary and consolidates the entity. Details include the arrangement's 15-year term and quarterly payments of $183 million received from Cardinal.

3. **Other Variable Interest Holders** - VIEs where the Company is not the primary beneficiary, including hedge fund investments ($589 million), private equity investments ($707 million), and real estate partnerships ($241 million) as of December 31, 2022.

4. **Related Party Transactions** - Equity method investments in SureScripts (clinical health information network) and Heartland Healthcare Services (LTC pharmacies), plus charitable contributions to the CVS Health Foundation.

5. **Discontinued Operations** - Retained lease guarantees from business dispositions completed between 1995-1997, with immaterial results for 2022 and 2021, and a $12 million loss in 2020.

6. **New Accounting Pronouncements** - Adoption of ASU 2018-12 regarding long-duration insurance contracts effective January 1, 2023, expected to increase liability for future policy benefits by approximately $1 billion.

nodes.title: Discontinued Operations
nodes.summary:
# Main Points Covered in Partial Document

1. **Equity Method Investments**: The company holds immaterial equity investments in SureScripts and Heartland Healthcare Services, LLC. Heartland operates long-term care pharmacies and purchased $87 million, $79 million, and $77 million in pharmaceutical inventory in 2022, 2021, and 2020 respectively.

2. **Charitable Contributions**: The company made charitable donations to the CVS Health Foundation of $25 million in 2022 and $50 million in both 2021 and 2020, supporting health, education, and community programs.

3. **Discontinued Operations**: The company retained lease guarantees for former subsidiaries (Linens 'n Things and Bob's Stores) that filed for bankruptcy. Discontinued operations were immaterial in 2022 and 2021, with a net loss of $9 million (after tax benefit) in 2020.

4. **New Accounting Standard Adoption**: The company adopted ASU 2018-12 regarding long-duration insurance contracts effective January 1, 2023. This standard requires annual review of cash flow assumptions, quarterly updates to discount rates, and changes to deferred acquisition cost amortization. The adoption is expected to increase the liability for future policy benefits by approximately $1 billion with a corresponding adjustment to accumulated other comprehensive income.

2. **Charitable Contributions**: The company made charitable contributions to the CVS Health Foundation of $25 million in 2022 and $50 million in both 2021 and 2020, recorded as operating expenses.

3. **Discontinued Operations**: The company retained lease guarantees on former subsidiaries (Linens 'n Things and Bob's Stores) that filed for bankruptcy. Results from discontinued operations were immaterial in 2022 and 2021, with a net loss of $9 million (after tax benefit) in 2020.

The adoption is expected to increase the liability for future policy benefits by approximately $1 billion with a corresponding adjustment to accumulated other comprehensive income.

nodes.title: Acquisitions, Divestitures and Asset Sales
nodes.summary:
# Description of Partial Document: Acquisitions, Divestitures and Asset Sales

## Main Points Covered:

**Proposed Acquisitions:**
- **Oak Street Health Acquisition**: Definitive merger agreement entered February 7, 2023, to acquire all outstanding shares for $39.00 per share in cash, totaling approximately $10.6 billion. Subject to stockholder approval, regulatory approval, and Hart-Scott-Rodino Act requirements. Expected to close in 2023. Includes termination fees of $500 million or $300 million depending on circumstances.

- **Signify Health Acquisition**: Definitive merger agreement entered September 2, 2022, to acquire all outstanding shares for $30.50 per share in cash, totaling approximately $8 billion. Approved by both boards and Signify Health stockholders. Received DOJ second request in October 2022. Expected to close in Q2 2023. Includes termination fees of $380 million or $228 million depending on circumstances.

**Assets Held for Sale:**
- **Omnicare Long-Term Care (LTC) Business**: Committed to sale in Q3 2022. Recorded $2.5 billion loss in Q3 2022 and $12 million additional loss in Q4 2022. Total assets held for sale of $908 million and liabilities of $228 million as of December 31, 2022.

**Completed Divestitures:**
- **bswift** (November 2022): Sold for approximately $735 million with $250 million pre-tax gain
- **PayFlex** (June 2022): Sold for approximately $775 million with $225 million pre-tax gain
- **Thailand Health Care Business** (March-Q2 2022): Recorded $41 million loss on assets held for sale
- **Workers' Compensation Business** (July 2020): Sold for approximately $850 million with $269 million pre-tax gain
- **International Health Care Benefits Renewal Rights** (May 2022): Sold renewal rights for approximately 200,000 international medical members

- Termination fees: $500 million if Company terminates without regulatory approval; $300 million if Oak Street Health accepts superior proposal

**Signify Health Acquisition:**
- Definitive merger agreement entered September 2, 2022
- Purchase price: $30.50 per share in cash
- Total transaction value: approximately $8 billion
- Approved by both companies' boards and Signify Health stockholders
- Received DOJ "second request" for additional information on October 19, 2022
- Expected closing: second quarter of 2023
- Termination fees: $380 million if Company terminates without regulatory approval; $228 million if Signify Health accepts superior proposal

## Asset Divestiture

**Omnicare Long-Term Care (LTC) Business:**
- Classified as held for sale in Q3 2022 as non-strategic asset
- Recorded $2.5 billion loss on assets held for sale in Q3 2022
- Additional $12 million loss recorded in Q4 2022
- Losses primarily reflect write-down of customer contracts/relationships intangible assets
- LTC business operating income was immaterial for 2020-2022

nodes.title: Assets Held For Sale
nodes.summary:
# Main Points Covered in Document Section 2: Acquisitions, Divestitures and Asset Sales

## Proposed Acquisitions
- **Oak Street Health Acquisition**: Definitive merger agreement entered February 7, 2023, to acquire all outstanding shares for $39.00 per share in cash, totaling approximately $10.6 billion. Subject to stockholder approval, regulatory approval, and Hart-Scott-Rodino Act requirements. Expected to close in 2023. Includes termination fees of $500 million (if regulatory approval not obtained) or $300 million (if Oak Street Health accepts superior proposal).

- **Signify Health Acquisition**: Definitive merger agreement entered September 2, 2022, to acquire all outstanding shares for $30.50 per share in cash, totaling approximately $8 billion. Approved by both boards and Signify Health stockholders. Received DOJ second request in October 2022. Expected to close in Q2 2023. Includes termination fees of $380 million (if regulatory approval not obtained) or $228 million (if Signify Health accepts superior proposal).

## Assets Held for Sale
- **Omnicare Long-Term Care Business**: Classified as held for sale in Q3 2022 with $2.5 billion loss recorded, plus additional $12 million loss in Q4 2022. Total assets held for sale of $908 million and liabilities of $228 million as of December 31, 2022.

## Completed Divestitures
- **bswift**: Sold November 2022 for approximately $735 million with $250 million pre-tax gain
- **PayFlex**: Sold June 2022 for approximately $775 million with $225 million pre-tax gain
- **Thailand Health Care Business**: Sold Q2 2022 with $41 million loss recorded
- **Workers' Compensation Business**: Sold July 31, 2020 for approximately $850 million with $269 million pre-tax gain

nodes.title: Divestiture of bswift
nodes.summary:
# Description of Partial Document

This document section covers the company's asset divestiture activities and held-for-sale classifications. The main points include:

1. **LTC Business Classification**: The LTC business was classified as held for sale at December 31, 2022, but not as discontinued operations. Assets and liabilities were reported separately on the balance sheet, with total assets held for sale of $908 million and total liabilities of $228 million.

2. **bswift Divestiture (November 2022)**: The company sold its wholly-owned subsidiary bswift LLC for approximately $735 million. bswift provided software and services for benefits and HR administration. The sale generated a pre-tax gain of $250 million, recorded within the Health Care Benefits segment.

3. **PayFlex Divestiture (June 2022)**: PayFlex was sold for approximately $775 million. The business provided tax-advantaged account administration, COBRA administration, and special-member billing services. The sale resulted in a pre-tax gain of $225 million within the Health Care Benefits segment.

4. **Thailand Health Care Business Divestiture (March-Q2 2022)**: The company's international health care business in Thailand, comprising approximately 266,000 medical members, was sold. A $41 million loss was recorded on assets held for sale in Q1 2022, with the final sale closing in Q2 2022.

5. **Workers' Compensation Business Divestiture (July 2020)**: The company sold its Workers' Compensation business for approximately $850 million, generating a pre-tax gain of $269 million.

nodes.title: International Health Care Benefits Renewal Rights Asset Sale
nodes.summary:
International Health Care Benefits Renewal Rights Asset Sale

In May 2022, the Company sold the renewal rights of approximately 200,000 international medical members outside of the Americas, Thailand and India in
connection with an Asset Purchase Agreement. As part of this agreement, the Company will introduce and help migrate these existing international medical
members to the purchaser upon renewal. The Company expects the migration process to occur between July 2022 and November 2023. The Company ceased
writing any new or renewal business for international medical members outside of the Americas during the fourth quarter of 2022. The consideration received
related to this agreement was not material.

# Description of Partial Document: Investments Section

## Main Points Covered:

**1. Investment Portfolio Overview**
- Total investments decreased from $26.142 billion (2021) to $23.891 billion (2022)
- Investments categorized into current and long-term portions

**2. Debt Securities Analysis**

- Detailed breakdown by security type: U.S. government securities, municipal securities, corporate securities, foreign securities, mortgage-backed securities, asset-backed securities, and preferred securities
- Significant unrealized losses in 2022 ($1.840 billion) primarily due to interest rate increases rather than credit quality deterioration
- Maturity schedule provided showing distribution across time periods

**3. Mortgage Loans Portfolio**
- Total mortgage loans of $1.044 billion (2022) and $902 billion (2021)
- Loans collateralized by commercial real estate
- Credit quality assessment using 7-category internal rating system
- Scheduled principal repayments provided through 2027 and beyond

**4. Net Investment Income**
- Gross investment income of $1.201 billion (2022) from debt securities, mortgage loans, and other investments
- Net investment income of $838 million after expenses and realized capital losses
- Significant realized capital losses of $320 million in 2022, primarily from yield-related impairments

**5. Fair Value Measurement Framework**
- Introduction to three-level fair value hierarchy (Level 1, 2, and 3) based on GAAP standards
- Valuation methodologies for different asset types including cash equivalents and debt securities
- Classification criteria based on availability of quoted market prices and observable market data

nodes.summary:
# Summary of Investment Portfolio Section

This section provides a comprehensive overview of the company's investment holdings and composition:

## Key Points Covered:

1. **Total Investment Summary**: Reports total investments of $23.891 billion (2022) and $26.142 billion (2021), broken down into current and long-term categories across three investment types: debt securities, mortgage loans, and other investments.

2. **Investment Composition**: 
   - Debt securities available for sale represent the largest portion ($20.280 billion in 2022)
   - Mortgage loans totaling $1.044 billion
   - Other investments of $2.567 billion

3. **Debt Securities Breakdown**: Detailed analysis of debt securities by type, including:
   - U.S. government securities
   - Municipal securities
   - U.S. corporate securities
   - Foreign securities
   - Mortgage-backed securities (residential and commercial)
   - Asset-backed securities
   - Redeemable preferred securities

4. **Fair Value Analysis**: Presents amortized cost, allowance for credit losses, unrealized gains/losses, and fair values for all debt securities in both 2022 and 2021.

5. **Maturity Schedule**: Shows debt securities distribution by contractual maturity dates, ranging from less than one year to greater than ten years.

6. **Mortgage-Backed and Asset-Backed Securities Details**: Describes credit quality ratings, duration metrics, underlying collateral types, and valuation inputs for these specialized securities.

7. **Special Investments**: Notes $331 million in legally segregated investments related to a 2012 group annuity contract conversion.

nodes.title: Mortgage-Backed and Other Asset-Backed Securities
nodes.summary:
# Main Points Covered in the Partial Document

## Debt Securities Portfolio
- Details of debt securities holdings at December 31, 2022, with total amortized cost of $22,063 million and fair value of $20,280 million
- Breakdown by maturity dates: securities maturing in less than one year through greater than ten years
- Composition includes residential mortgage-backed securities ($845M amortized cost), commercial mortgage-backed securities ($1,172M), and other asset-backed securities ($2,940M)

## Mortgage-Backed and Asset-Backed Securities Quality
- Residential mortgage-backed securities issued by government-sponsored entities (GNMA, FNMA, FHLMC) with AAA ratings and 5.7-year weighted average duration
- Commercial mortgage-backed securities with AAA ratings and 5.6-year weighted average duration
- Other asset-backed securities (auto loans, credit cards, home equity) with AA ratings and 1.0-year weighted average duration

## Unrealized Capital Losses Analysis
- Comprehensive table showing 12,284 debt securities with $18,492 million fair value and $1,840 million in unrealized losses as of December 31, 2022
- Comparison with 2021 data showing significant increase in unrealized losses
- Breakdown by security type and duration of loss position (less than 12 months vs. greater than 12 months)

## Management Assessment
- Company concluded unrealized losses were primarily caused by interest rate increases, not credit quality deterioration
- Company does not intend to sell these securities and believes recovery of amortized cost basis is likely
- Securities are performing assets generating investment income

## Mortgage Loans Portfolio
- Commercial real estate-collateralized mortgage loans activity in 2022 and 2021
- Credit quality assessment methodology using internally-developed 7-category rating scale based on loan-to-value ratios, cash flow, property condition, and borrower creditworthiness

# Description of Partial Document

This document section covers CVS Health's financial reporting on capital gains/losses, fair value measurements, and goodwill accounting:

## Capital Gains and Losses
- Capital gains and losses from equity securities held during 2022 were immaterial
- Details provided on proceeds from sales of available-for-sale debt securities for 2022, 2021, and 2020, showing gross realized gains and losses

## Fair Value Measurements (Note 4)
- Explains the three-level GAAP fair value hierarchy used to measure financial assets and liabilities:
  - **Level 1**: Unadjusted quoted prices in active markets
  - **Level 2**: Observable market data other than Level 1 inputs
  - **Level 3**: Unobservable inputs based on company assumptions
- Describes valuation methodologies for specific asset classes (cash equivalents, debt securities, equity securities)
- Provides detailed tables of financial assets measured at fair value as of December 31, 2022 and 2021, broken down by hierarchy level
- Includes reconciliation of Level 3 financial assets showing beginning balances, purchases, sales, transfers, and ending balances
- Discusses financial instruments not measured at fair value but carried at adjusted cost
- Covers Separate Accounts assets related to pension products, which are maintained at fair value with corresponding liabilities

## Goodwill and Intangibles (Note 5)
- Summarizes goodwill changes by business segment (Health Care Benefits, Pharmacy Services, Retail/LTC) for 2022 and 2021
- Notes 2022 goodwill decrease of $971 million due to divestitures (bswift, PayFlex, Thailand business)
- Documents 2021 goodwill impairment of $431 million in the LTC reporting unit due to COVID-19 pandemic impacts and lower facility admissions
- Reports no goodwill impairment in 2022 annual testing
- Cumulative goodwill impairments total $6.6 billion as of December 31, 2022 and 2021

nodes.title: Financial Instruments Measured at Fair Value on the Consolidated Balance Sheets
nodes.summary:
# Description of Partial Document

This document section covers capital gains/losses and fair value measurement practices for CVS Health's financial instruments:

## Main Points:

1. **Capital Gains and Losses (2022)**: Capital gains and losses from equity securities held as of December 31, 2022 were immaterial. For available-for-sale debt securities (excluding experience-rated products), the company reported proceeds from sales of $4.2 billion in 2022, with gross realized capital gains of $24 million and losses of $177 million.

2. **Fair Value Measurement Framework**: The company explains its approach to measuring financial assets and liabilities at fair value, including those impacting net income and other comprehensive income.

3. **Fair Value Hierarchy**: Three-level classification system for valuation inputs:
   - **Level 1**: Unadjusted quoted prices in active markets
   - **Level 2**: Observable market data (similar asset prices, interest rates, credit spreads)
   - **Level 3**: Unobservable inputs based on company assumptions

4. **Valuation Methodologies**: Detailed descriptions of how specific asset classes are valued:
   - Cash equivalents (Level 1 or 2 based on market activity)
   - Debt securities (Level 1 for Treasury securities; Level 2 using matrix pricing; Level 3 for private placements)
   - Equity securities (Level 1 for publicly traded; Level 3 for privately placed)

5. **Fair Value Tables**: Comprehensive balance sheet data showing financial assets measured at fair value as of December 31, 2022 and 2021, totaling $33.4 billion and $32.8 billion respectively, broken down by hierarchy level and asset type.

6. **Level 3 Changes**: Reconciliation of Level 3 asset movements during 2022 and 2021, showing beginning balances, purchases, sales, transfers, and ending balances.

## Financial Instruments Not Measured at Fair Value
- Details carrying values and estimated fair values for assets and liabilities recorded at adjusted cost or contract value
- Provides a fair value hierarchy breakdown (Levels 1, 2, and 3) for:
  - **Assets**: Mortgage loans and equity securities
  - **Liabilities**: Investment contract liabilities (with and without fixed maturity) and long-term debt
- Includes comparative data for both 2022 and 2021
- Notes that equity securities are cost-method investments in unlisted companies where fair value estimation is impractical
- References long-term debt classified as liabilities held for sale

## Separate Accounts Measured at Fair Value
- Explains that Separate Accounts relate to large case pension products where contract holders bear investment risk
- Describes the legal segregation of these assets from the company's other businesses
- Clarifies that investment income, capital gains/losses, deposits, and withdrawals on Separate Accounts do not flow through consolidated financial statements
- Details that Separate Accounts assets include debt and equity securities valued using standard methodologies
- Explains the valuation of common/collective trust investments at net asset value (NAV), classified as Level 2 fair value measurements

# Main Points Covered in the Partial Document

## Separate Accounts Measured at Fair Value
- Explains that Separate Accounts relate to large case pension products where contract holders bear investment risk

- Assets are legally segregated and not subject to claims from other company businesses
- Separate Accounts transactions are excluded from consolidated financial statements
- Details composition of Separate Accounts assets including debt securities, equity securities, and common/collective trusts
- Provides fair value tables for Separate Accounts assets at December 31, 2022 and 2021
- Notes no transfers of Separate Accounts assets into or out of Level 3 during 2022 and 2021

- Documents goodwill impairment of $431 million in 2021 related to LTC reporting unit
- Details 2022 goodwill decrease of $971 million due to divestitures (bswift, PayFlex, Thailand business)
- Explains 2021 LTC impairment drivers including lower admissions, customer losses, and COVID-19 pandemic impacts
- Reports no goodwill impairment in 2022 annual testing
- Notes cumulative goodwill impairments of $6.6 billion as of December 31, 2022 and 2021

# Description of Intangible Assets Document

This document provides a comprehensive summary of the Company's intangible assets as of December 31, 2022 and 2021. The main points covered include:

**Intangible Asset Categories:**
- Trademarks (indefinite-lived): $10.5 billion, not subject to amortization

- Technology: Fully amortized with zero net carrying amount

- Projected annual amortization expense ranges from $1.2 billion to $1.6 billion over the next five years (2023-2027)
- Weighted average life of total intangible assets is 13.9 years (2022)

nodes.title: Leases
nodes.summary:
# Description of Partial Document: Leases

## Main Points Covered:

1. **Lease Portfolio Overview**: The company leases retail stores, mail order facilities, distribution centers, corporate offices, and equipment under operating or finance leases, with initial terms ranging from 3-25 years. Special pharmacy lease arrangements exist with another retail chain.

2. **Net Lease Cost Analysis**: Detailed breakdown of lease costs for 2020-2022, including operating lease costs ($2.6-2.7 billion annually), finance lease costs (amortization and interest), short-term and variable lease costs, and sublease income deductions.

3. **Cash Flow Information**: Summary of cash paid for lease obligations, including operating cash flows for leases and financing cash flows for principal portions, plus right-of-use assets obtained through lease exchanges.

4. **Balance Sheet Metrics**: Supplemental balance sheet information showing operating and finance lease right-of-use assets, current and long-term lease liabilities, weighted average remaining lease terms (12.2 years for operating, 19.4 years for finance), and discount rates.

5. **Lease Maturity Schedule**: Future lease payment obligations broken down by year through 2027 and thereafter, totaling $26.3 billion, with imputed interest calculations.

6. **Sale-Leaseback Transactions**: Description of financing strategy through property sales and leasebacks, with no transactions in 2022-2021 and $101 million in proceeds during 2020.

7. **Office Real Estate Optimization**: $117 million in charges recorded in Q4 2022 related to vacating corporate office spaces due to flexible work arrangements.

8. **Store Impairment Charges**: Detailed explanation of impairment testing methodology and $1.4 billion in charges recorded in Q4 2021 related to the closure of approximately 900 retail stores announced by the Board.

nodes.title: Sale-Leaseback Transactions
nodes.summary:
# Main Points Covered in the Partial Document

## Sale-Leaseback Transactions
- The company finances store development through sale-leaseback transactions where properties are sold at net book value
- Resulting leases are accounted for as operating leases
- No sale-leaseback transactions occurred in 2022 or 2021
- In 2020, proceeds totaled $101 million with gains of $3 million

## Office Real Estate Optimization Charges
- In Q4 2022, the company evaluated corporate office real estate in response to new flexible work arrangements
- Determined certain leased corporate office spaces would be vacated and abandoned
- Recorded $117 million in optimization charges, consisting of $71 million in operating lease right-of-use asset write-downs and $44 million in property and equipment charges
- Charges distributed across Health Care Benefits, Corporate/Other, and Pharmacy Services segments

## Store Impairment Charges
- The company evaluates retail store assets for impairment at the individual store level
- Uses a two-step process: comparing carrying amount to undiscounted future cash flows, then calculating impairment loss using fair value
- Fair value estimates consider historical results, operating trends, sales, profitability, and cash flow forecasts
- In Q4 2021, the Board authorized closing approximately 900 retail stores (300 per year from 2022-2024)
- Recorded $1.4 billion store impairment charge in Q4 2021, including $1.1 billion in operating lease right-of-use asset write-downs and $261 million in property and equipment charges within the Retail/LTC segment

nodes.title: Office Real Estate Optimization Charges
nodes.summary:
# Main Points Covered in the Partial Document

## Sale-Leaseback Transactions
- The company finances store development through sale-leaseback transactions where properties are sold at net book value
- Resulting leases are accounted for as operating leases
- No sale-leaseback transactions occurred in 2022 or 2021
- In 2020, proceeds totaled $101 million with gains of $3 million

## Office Real Estate Optimization Charges
- In Q4 2022, the company evaluated corporate office real estate in response to new flexible work arrangements
- The company decided to vacate and abandon certain leased corporate office spaces
- Total optimization charges of $117 million were recorded, consisting of $71 million in operating lease right-of-use assets and $44 million in property and equipment
- Charges were distributed across Health Care Benefits, Corporate/Other, and Pharmacy Services segments

## Store Impairment Charges
- The company evaluates retail store assets for impairment at the individual store level
- Impairment testing compares carrying amounts to estimated future cash flows (undiscounted), then to fair value (discounted)
- Fair value estimates consider historical results, operating trends, sales, profitability, and forecasts
- In Q4 2021, the Board authorized closing approximately 900 retail stores (300 per year from 2022-2024)
- A $1.4 billion store impairment charge was recorded in Q4 2021, consisting of $1.1 billion in operating lease right-of-use assets and $261 million in property and equipment
- Post-impairment fair values were $356 million and $185 million respectively

# Description of Partial Document: Health Care Costs Payable

## Main Points Covered:

1. **IBNR Liability Estimation**: The company estimates Incurred But Not Reported (IBNR) liabilities primarily using trend and completion factors, rather than claim frequency data. Claim frequency information is not disclosed due to inconsistencies across multiple claims processing systems.

2. **Claims Development Tables**: The document presents incurred and cumulative paid health care claims data as of December 31, 2022, net of reinsurance, broken down by date of service for 2021 and 2022, showing total incurred claims of $131.4 billion and total paid claims of $121.7 billion.

3. **IBNR Liabilities**: As of December 31, 2022, IBNR liabilities plus expected development on reported claims totaled approximately $7.8 billion, with substantially all related to the current calendar year.

4. **Balance Sheet Reconciliation**: The document reconciles the claims development tables to the health care costs payable liability on the consolidated balance sheet, totaling $10.4 billion as of December 31, 2022.

5. **Three-Year Trend Analysis**: A reconciliation table shows changes in health care costs payable for 2022, 2021, and 2020, including beginning balances, incurred costs, claims paid, and ending balances.

6. **Prior Year Development**: The company experienced favorable prior year claims development, with reductions of $654 million (2022), $788 million (2021), and $429 million (2020), primarily due to lower health care cost trends and faster claim submission times than originally estimated.

1. **Long-term Debt Summary**: Comprehensive table detailing the Company's senior notes outstanding as of December 31, 2022 and 2021, including interest rates ranging from 1.3% to 6.75%, with maturity dates spanning from 2023 to 2050. Total debt principal decreased from $56.743 billion (2021) to $52.753 billion (2022).

2. **Debt Composition**: Long-term debt of $50.479 billion (2022) after adjusting for debt premiums, discounts, deferred financing costs, and current portions. Includes finance lease liabilities of $1.465 billion.

3. **Debt Maturity Schedule**: Five-year repayment schedule showing principal due annually from 2023-2027 and thereafter, with significant amounts due in 2025 ($3.785 billion) and beyond 2027 ($37.192 billion).

4. **Short-term Borrowings**: Company maintains $6.0 billion in back-up revolving credit facilities (expiring 2025-2027) with no outstanding commercial paper or borrowings. Federal Home Loan Bank membership provides $915 million additional borrowing capacity.

5. **Debt Management Activities**: Details of par call redemptions in 2022 totaling $4.149 billion and early extinguishments of debt from 2020-2021, including cash tender offers and associated premiums and losses.

6. **Debt Covenants**: Company maintains customary restrictive financial and operating covenants with no credit rating acceleration clauses and confirmed compliance as of December 31, 2022.

**Section 9: Pension Plans and Other Postretirement Benefits**

1. **Defined Contribution Plans**: Company sponsors multiple 401(k) savings plans covering all eligible employees with matching contributions. Includes nonqualified deferred compensation plans for key employees.

2. **Company Contributions**: Annual contributions to defined contribution plans were $567 million (2022), $552 million (2021), and $520 million (2020).

# Description of Partial Document

This document section provides a comprehensive overview of the Company's defined benefit pension plans and their financial status. The main points covered include:

- The Company maintains a tax-qualified defined benefit pension plan (frozen in 2010) and a nonqualified supplemental pension plan (frozen in 2007)

## Pension Benefit Obligation and Plan Assets Analysis
- Detailed reconciliation of changes in pension benefit obligation and plan assets for 2022 and 2021

- Plan assets decreased from $6,677 million (2021) to $5,346 million (2022), reflecting negative actual returns of $968 million
- Overall funded status remained positive at $606 million in 2022 (down from $668 million in 2021)

- Net pension asset position of $606 million as of December 31, 2022

- Three-year comparison (2020-2022) of pension cost components
- The plans generated net periodic benefit income in all three years, with 2022 showing income of $173 million
- Key components include interest cost, expected return on plan assets, and amortization of actuarial losses

nodes.title: Pension Plan Assumptions
nodes.summary:
# Description of Partial Document

This document section outlines the pension plan assumptions and assets used by the Company to calculate its benefit obligations and periodic benefit costs.

## Main Points Covered:

**Pension Plan Assumptions:**
- The Company uses actuarial assumptions to determine benefit obligations and net periodic benefit income, with discount rates and expected return on plan assets being the most significant
- Discount rates are determined using a yield curve based on high-quality bonds as of the annual measurement date, with projected benefit payments discounted using rates consistent with liability cash flow maturity profiles
- Expected long-term return on plan assets is determined using the plan's target allocation and return expectations based on forecasted long-term capital market returns and inflationary outlook
- Other assumptions considered include mortality, interest crediting rates, termination and retirement rates, and cost of living adjustments

**Weighted Average Assumptions:**
- Discount rates increased significantly from 2.8% (2021) to 5.2% (2022) for benefit obligations
- Discount rates for net periodic benefit cost ranged from 1.8% to 2.9% across 2020-2022
- Expected long-term return on plan assets was 4.8% for 2021-2022, down from 6.3% in 2020

**Pension Plan Assets:**
- Assets primarily consist of debt and equity securities, common/collective trusts, and real estate investments
- Real estate investments are valued by independent third-party appraisers using income, cost, and sales comparison approaches (Level 3 classification)
- Private equity and hedge fund limited partnerships are valued using NAV per unit as a practical expedient to fair value

nodes.title: Pension Plan Assets
nodes.summary:
# Main Points Covered in Pension Plan Assumptions and Assets Document

## Pension Plan Assumptions
- **Discount Rates**: Determined using yield curves based on high-quality bonds as of the annual measurement date, with rates corresponding to projected benefit payment maturities. Discount rates increased from 2.8% (2021) to 5.2% (2022) for benefit obligations.
- **Expected Return on Plan Assets**: Long-term rate of return determined using target asset allocation and capital market forecasts, remaining at 4.8% for 2022 and 2021 (down from 6.3% in 2020).
- **Other Assumptions**: Mortality, interest crediting rates, termination/retirement rates, and cost of living adjustments are also considered.

## Pension Plan Assets Composition
- **Asset Types**: Debt securities, equity securities, real estate investments, common/collective trusts, and private equity/hedge fund partnerships.
- **Fair Value Hierarchy**: Assets classified as Level 1 (quoted prices), Level 2 (observable inputs), or Level 3 (unobservable inputs).
- **2022 Asset Allocation**: Total pension investments of $4.3 billion, with significant holdings in debt securities ($3.4 billion) and equity securities ($176 million).
- **2021 Asset Allocation**: Total pension investments of $5.8 billion, reflecting market value changes.

## Investment Strategy and Risk Management
- **Target Allocations (2022)**: 12% equity, 77% fixed income/debt, 5% real estate, 3% private equity, 3% hedge funds.
- **Investment Objectives**: Generate returns to meet future benefit obligations while managing investment and actuarial risks through diversified strategies.
- **Oversight**: Regular review by Investment Subcommittee with periodic rebalancing to target allocations.

## Cash Flows and Contributions
- **Company Contributions**: $27 million (2022), $78 million (2021), $25 million (2020).
- **Future Benefit Payments**: Estimated at $368-372 million annually for 2023-2027, with $1.8 billion projected for 2028-2032.
- **Multiemployer Plans**: Company contributes $20 million annually to multiemployer pension plans covering union-represented employees.

## Other Postretirement Benefits
- **Accumulated Obligation**: $159 million (2022) and $207 million (2021) for health care and life insurance benefits.
- **Net Periodic Costs**: $4 million (2022 and 2021), $12 million (2020).

nodes.title: Stock-Based Compensation Expense

# Description of Partial Document

This document excerpt covers two main sections of CVS Health Corporation's financial disclosures:

## Tax Examination Status and Unrecognized Tax Benefits
- CVS Health participates in the IRS's Compliance Assurance Process (CAP) program for collaborative tax issue resolution
- The IRS has completed examinations of federal income tax returns through 2013 and 2018-2019, with 2014-2017 substantially completed
- State and local tax examinations are ongoing with no material proposed adjustments as of December 31, 2022
- Most material state and local matters concluded through fiscal year 2015
- The company accrued approximately $112 million for interest and penalties related to unrecognized tax benefits as of December 31, 2022
- Total unrecognized tax benefits that would affect the effective tax rate amount to approximately $336 million

## Stock Incentive Plans and Compensation
- CVS Health operates under the 2017 Incentive Compensation Plan (ICP) with a maximum of 58 million shares reserved, with approximately 21 million shares available for future grants as of December 31, 2022
- Following the Aetna acquisition in November 2018, approximately 22 million Aetna shares were assumed and converted into approximately 32 million CVS Health shares
- Stock-based compensation is measured at grant date fair value and recognized over a requisite service period of three to five years
- Total stock-based compensation expenses were $447 million in 2022, $484 million in 2021, and $400 million in 2020, comprising restricted stock units, performance stock units, stock options, and stock appreciation rights

nodes.title: Restricted Stock Units and Performance Stock Units
nodes.summary:
# Main Points Covered in the Partial Document

## Restricted Stock Units (RSUs) and Performance Stock Units (PSUs)

- RSUs and PSUs are nonvested share awards requiring no employee payment
- RSU fair value is based on CVS Health common stock market price on grant date, recognized straight-line over vesting period
- Employees receive one share per RSU granted, net of taxes, upon vesting
- PSUs include performance vesting conditions tied to company-achieved performance goals over three-year periods
- Some PSUs contain market vesting conditions based on CVS Health stock performance relative to comparator groups, valued using Monte Carlo simulation
- As of December 31, 2022: $619 million in unrecognized compensation cost expected to be recognized over 2.1 years weighted-average period
- 2022 vested RSU/PSU fair value: $328 million (compared to $406 million in 2021 and $229 million in 2020)
- Year-end 2022 activity: 12,681 thousand nonvested units outstanding with $80.25 weighted average grant date fair value

## Stock Options and Stock Appreciation Rights (SARs)

- Stock options awarded at fair value on grant date using Black-Scholes pricing model
- Options generally exercisable over four-year period; expire seven years (pre-2018 grants) or ten years (post-2018 grants) after grant date
- Compensation recognized straight-line over requisite service period
- Aetna SARs converted to CVS Health SARs upon acquisition, settled in company stock net of taxes
- SARs valued using Black-Scholes model, exercisable over three-year period, expire ten years after grant date
- No SARs granted after Aetna Acquisition

## Main Points:

nodes.title: Other Comprehensive Income (Loss)
nodes.summary:
# Description of Section 13: Other Comprehensive Income (Loss)

This section presents a detailed breakdown of accumulated other comprehensive income (loss) activity in shareholders' equity for the years 2022, 2021, and 2020. The main points covered include:

1. **Net Unrealized Investment Gains (Losses)**: Tracks changes in unrealized gains/losses on investments, showing a significant decline from a $778 million balance at the end of 2021 to a $(1,501) million loss at the end of 2022, primarily due to a $(2,972) million pretax loss before reclassifications.

2. **Foreign Currency Translation Adjustments**: Minimal activity with balances declining from $7 million in 2020 to zero by 2022.

3. **Net Cash Flow Hedges**: Relatively stable activity with balances ranging from $222-248 million across the three-year period, reflecting hedging activities related to interest rate and currency exposures.

4. **Pension and Other Postretirement Benefits**: Shows accumulated losses increasing from $(55) million in 2020 to $(203) million in 2022, driven by actuarial losses and remeasurement adjustments.

5. **Total Accumulated Other Comprehensive Income (Loss)**: Demonstrates a significant swing from a positive $1,414 million position in 2020 to a negative $(1,465) million position in 2022, primarily driven by investment losses.

The section also includes footnotes explaining how reclassified amounts are presented in the consolidated statements of operations and notes regarding expected future reclassifications.

nodes.title: Lease Guarantees
nodes.summary:
# Main Points Covered in Partial Document

## Guaranteed Benefit Obligations and Lease Guarantees
- The Company had no additional liability requirements for guaranteed benefit obligations as of December 31, 2022
- The Company maintains lease guarantees for 67 store leases from subsidiaries (Bob's Stores, Linens 'n Things, and Marshalls) sold or spun off between 1995-1997, with terms extending through 2034
- Although indemnification agreements exist with purchasers, the Company remains liable if former subsidiaries or purchasers fail to meet lease obligations

## Guaranty Fund Assessments and Risk-Sharing Pools
- The Company participates in state insurance guaranty funds that assess insurers for obligations of insolvent insurance companies
- Assessments are based on the Company's health care premiums relative to other insurers in each state
- The Company has recorded liabilities for estimated future assessments related to Penn Treaty Network America Insurance Company's liquidation (placed in liquidation in March 2017)
- HMOs in certain states are subject to market stabilization and risk-sharing pool assessments based on incurred claims and membership demographics
- Total guaranty fund assessments liability was immaterial at December 31, 2022 and 2021

## Litigation and Regulatory Proceedings
- The Company is involved in numerous legal proceedings including litigation, arbitration, and government investigations
- Regulatory bodies involved include CMS, state insurance departments, DOJ, state Attorneys General, DEA, FTC, and other governmental authorities
- Legal proceedings can be expensive and disruptive, particularly securities and class action litigation

nodes.title: Litigation and Regulatory Proceedings
nodes.summary:
# Main Points Covered in the Partial Document

## Guarantees and Contingent Liabilities
- Lease guarantees on 67 store leases from subsidiaries sold between 1995-1997 (Bob's Stores, Linens 'n Things, Marshalls), extending through 2034
- Guaranty fund assessments for insolvent insurance companies under state guaranty fund laws
- Penn Treaty Network America Insurance Company liquidation and related assessments

## Litigation and Legal Proceedings Overview
- Company involved in numerous legal proceedings including litigation, arbitration, government investigations, and regulatory audits
- Accruals recorded only when loss is probable and reasonably estimable
- No material individual or aggregate accruals for outstanding legal matters

## Major Litigation Categories

**Usual and Customary Pricing Litigation:** Lawsuits alleging retail pharmacies overcharged for prescription drugs by not submitting correct usual and customary prices

**PBM Litigation and Investigations:** Multiple lawsuits and investigations regarding drug pricing, rebate arrangements, and PBM practices by state Attorneys General, FTC, and other regulators

**Controlled Substances/Opioid Litigation:** 
- Consolidated multidistrict litigation (MDL No. 2804) in Ohio federal court
- $651 million judgment in November 2021 (under appeal)
- Settlement agreements with Florida ($484 million), New Mexico, West Virginia, and Cherokee Nation
- December 2022 settlement agreement with state Attorneys General for approximately $4.3 billion in remediation plus $625 million in attorneys' fees over 10 years
- December 2022 tribal settlement agreement for $113 million plus $18 million in attorneys' fees

**Prescription Processing Litigation:** Cases involving improper prescription filling and False Claims Act violations

**Provider Proceedings:** Class actions and individual lawsuits regarding out-of-network provider payment practices and claim administration

**CMS Actions:** Risk Adjustment Data Validation (RADV) audits of Medicare Advantage plans; new RADV Audit Rule issued January 30, 2023

**Medicare and Medicaid Investigations:** Civil Investigative Demands regarding patient chart review processes and prescription drug reimbursements

**Stockholder Matters:** Securities class actions and ERISA claims related to LTC business unit performance; Delaware inspection demand regarding opioid-related fiduciary duties

## Other Legal and Regulatory Matters
- General contractual, employment, intellectual property, and product liability litigation
- Government contract protests affecting Medicaid and other government contracts
- Heightened regulatory review of health care industry practices including premium rates, utilization management, provider networks, and claim payment practices

## Segment Reporting
- Three operating segments: Health Care Benefits, Pharmacy Services, and Retail/LTC
- Federal government revenues represent 18% of consolidated total revenues (primarily Medicare contracts)
- Performance evaluated based on adjusted operating income excluding amortization of intangible assets

nodes.title: Usual and Customary Pricing Litigation
nodes.summary:
# Main Points Covered in the Partial Document

## Legal Proceedings and Contingencies
- The Company faces various legal matters including class actions, qui tam actions, and government investigations with uncertain outcomes and substantial costs
- The Company records accruals for legal matters only when loss is probable and reasonably estimable on a quarterly basis
- No individual accruals for outstanding legal matters are material to the Company's financial condition

## Usual and Customary Pricing Litigation
- Multiple lawsuits allege the Company's retail pharmacies overcharged for prescription drugs by submitting incorrect usual and customary prices
- Plaintiffs include plan members, private payors, government payors, and shareholders; some cases are certified class actions
- The Company is defending against these claims, including a shareholder derivative complaint

## PBM (Pharmacy Benefit Manager) Litigation and Investigations
- The Company faces multiple lawsuits from state Attorneys General, governmental subdivisions, and putative class actions regarding drug pricing and rebate arrangements with drug manufacturers
- Allegations center on rebate agreements causing inflated drug prices
- The Company is under investigation by the FTC and multiple state Attorneys Generals regarding PBM practices, pricing, and rebates
- A specific qui tam case (United States ex rel. Behnke v. CVS Caremark Corporation) alleges the Company submitted misrepresented Medicare Part D data regarding true prices paid to pharmacies
