nodes.title: Controlled Substances Litigation, Audits and Subpoenas
nodes.summary:
# Main Points Covered in the Partial Document

## Controlled Substances Litigation
- The company is a defendant in a consolidated multidistrict litigation (MDL No. 2804) in the U.S. District Court for the Northern District of Ohio concerning prescription opioid abuse claims dating back to the early 2000s
- Hundreds of federal cases and numerous state court cases name the company as a defendant
- The company has received subpoenas and requests for information from state Attorneys General and regulators

## Opioid Settlement Agreements
- In November 2021, the company was found liable in an Ohio trial with a judgment of $651 million over 15 years (under appeal)
- Multiple state settlements reached: Florida ($484 million over 18 years), New Mexico, West Virginia, and Cherokee Nation
- In December 2022, the company agreed to a major settlement with state Attorneys General and the Plaintiffs' Executive Committee for approximately $4.3 billion in remediation and $625 million in attorneys' fees over 10 years
- A separate tribal settlement agreement for $113 million plus $18 million in attorneys' fees over 10 years
- The company recorded pre-tax charges of $5.2 billion and $99 million in 2022 related to these settlements

## Federal Investigations
- The Department of Justice served administrative subpoenas and a Civil Investigative Demand (CID) regarding opioid practices and potential violations of the Controlled Substances Act and False Claims Act

## Prescription Processing Litigation
- Multiple lawsuits and investigations concerning prescription processing practices, including qui tam cases alleging improper prescription filling and violations of the False Claims Act
- Investigations by the California Department of Insurance and U.S. Attorney's Office regarding pharmacy practices

## Provider Proceedings
- Class action and individual lawsuits alleging the company underpaid providers and members for out-of-network services and failed to timely process claims

nodes.title: Prescription Processing Litigation and Investigations
nodes.summary:
# Main Points Covered in Partial Document

## Opioid-Related Claims and Settlements
- Company concluded that settlement of opioid claims by governmental entities and tribes is probable with reasonably estimated losses
- Recorded pre-tax charges of $5.2 billion (Q3 2022) and $99 million (Q4 2022)
- Expected cash impact in 2023-2024 to be less than $500 million annually
- Settlement is in early phases with inherent uncertainties and potential for material differences from accrual
- Company unable to estimate full range of possible loss due to uncertainties in scope and participation

## Government Investigations on Opioids
- DOJ served administrative subpoena in January 2020 regarding prescription opioid practices at CVS pharmacy locations
- DOJ served Civil Investigative Demand (CID) in January 2022 on similar subjects
- Company cooperating and providing documents and information

## Prescription Processing Litigation
- Multiple lawsuits and investigations concerning prescription processing practices
- U.S. ex rel. Bassan et al. v. Omnicare case alleging improper prescription filling beyond one year
- U.S. ex rel. Gill et al. v. CVS Health case involving false claims allegations and escheatment issues
- California Department of Insurance subpoena regarding Omnicare pharmacies' cycle fill process
- U.S. Attorney's Office CID regarding improper insulin claims submitted to Medicare Part D instead of Part B

## Provider Payment Proceedings
- Class action and individual lawsuits regarding payment practices for out-of-network provider services
- Allegations of underpayment to health plan members and providers for out-of-network services including COVID-19 testing
- Claims of failure to timely or appropriately pay or administer claims and benefits

nodes.title: Provider Proceedings
nodes.summary:
# Summary of Main Points

This document covers CVS Health's significant legal and regulatory matters:

## Opioid-Related Claims and Litigation
- The company recorded $5.2 billion in pre-tax charges (Q3 2022) and $99 million (Q4 2022) for opioid settlement obligations
- Expected cash impact of less than $500 million annually in 2023-2024
- Settlement is in early phases with inherent uncertainties; ultimate loss may differ materially
- DOJ served administrative subpoena (January 2020) and Civil Investigative Demand (January 2022) regarding opioid prescription practices

## Prescription Processing Litigation and Investigations
- Multiple lawsuits alleging improper prescription filling practices at Omnicare pharmacies beyond valid prescription periods
- Qui tam cases in New York and Illinois involving False Claims Act violations
- California Department of Insurance investigation into cycle fill processes for assisted living facilities
- Federal investigation regarding improper Medicare Part D vs. Part B insulin claim submissions

## Provider Payment Proceedings
- Class action and individual lawsuits alleging underpayment to health plan members and out-of-network providers
- Allegations of improper post-payment audit and collection practices
- Regulatory investigations by state Attorneys General regarding out-of-network benefit payment practices
- Risk of additional litigation and regulatory action

## CMS Risk Adjustment Data Validation (RADV) Audits
- CMS conducts audits to validate coding practices and medical record documentation supporting risk-adjusted premium payments
- January 2023 final rule allows extrapolation of error rates without FFS Adjuster for payment years 2018 forward
- Company unable to predict which contracts will be audited or amounts of potential refunds
- Potential impact on Medicare Advantage premium revenue and bid submissions

nodes.title: CMS Actions
nodes.summary:
# Main Points Covered in the Partial Document

## Litigation and Regulatory Challenges

The document discusses multiple categories of legal and regulatory issues facing the company:

1. **Out-of-Network Benefit Practices**: The company faces litigation and regulatory investigations from state Attorneys General and federal regulators regarding its out-of-network benefit payment and administration practices, with potential for additional litigation.

2. **CMS Risk Adjustment Data Validation (RADV) Audits**: 
   - CMS conducts audits to validate coding practices and medical record documentation for Medicare Advantage plans
   - The company may be required to refund premium payments if risk-adjusted premiums are not properly supported
   - CMS revised its audit methodology in 2012 and again in January 2023, eliminating the FFS Adjuster and allowing extrapolation of error rates
   - The new methodology targets contracts with highest risk for overpayments based on statistical modeling
   - The company cannot predict which contracts will be audited or the amounts of potential refunds

3. **Medicare and Medicaid Investigations**: 
   - The company received Civil Investigative Demands (CIDs) from the DOJ regarding patient chart review processes for risk adjustment data
   - A CID from the Southern District of New York concerns possible false claims for Medicare Part D prescription drug reimbursements

4. **Stockholder Litigation**: Multiple class action and derivative complaints filed beginning in February 2019 alleging omissions and misrepresentations related to the company's Long-Term Care (LTC) business unit, with mixed outcomes in dismissals and appeals.

5. **ERISA Claims**: Two consolidated class actions alleging breach of fiduciary duties related to the Aetna 401(k) Plan's investment in company stock, with the company obtaining dismissal with prejudice in October 2022, though plaintiffs appealed.

6. **Other Legal Proceedings**: The company faces various other claims including bad faith, malpractice, claims processing issues, regulatory non-compliance, and anticompetitive practices.

nodes.title: Medicare and Medicaid CIDs
nodes.summary:
# Main Points Covered in the Partial Document

This document discusses legal, regulatory, and compliance matters affecting the company, including:

1. **Regulatory Audits and Refunds**: Potential material premium/fee refunds or adjustments from RADV (Risk Adjustment Data Validation), Public Exchange, and other CMS/OIG audits, including MLR rebate audits.

2. **Medicare and Medicaid Investigations**: 
   - DOJ Civil Division CIDs regarding patient chart review processes and risk adjustment data submissions under Medicare Parts C and D
   - SDNY CID from May 2017 concerning alleged false claims for Medicare Part D prescription drug reimbursements

3. **Securities Class Action Lawsuits**: Multiple federal securities lawsuits filed beginning February 2019 against the company and officers/directors, alleging omissions and misrepresentations related to the LTC (Long-Term Care) business unit performance, with mixed outcomes including one dismissal affirmed on appeal.

4. **ERISA Litigation**: Two consolidated class action lawsuits filed in 2020 alleging breach of fiduciary duties and prohibited transactions related to the Aetna 401(k) Plan's company stock investments, with the company obtaining a dismissal with prejudice in October 2022, though plaintiffs appealed.

5. **Delaware Shareholder Demand**: December 2021 demand for inspection of books and records related to potential fiduciary duty breaches concerning opioid-related matters.

6. **Other Legal Proceedings**: Various ongoing legal proceedings and government investigations involving claims related to bad faith, malpractice, claims processing, regulatory non-compliance, marketing misconduct, provider networks, and other contractual and employment matters.

nodes.title: Stockholder Matters
nodes.summary:
# Main Points Covered in the Partial Document

This document discusses legal, regulatory, and compliance matters affecting the company, including:

1. **Regulatory Audits and Refunds**: Potential material premium/fee refunds or adjustments from RADV (Risk Adjustment Data Validation), Public Exchange, and other CMS/OIG audits, including MLR rebate audits.

2. **Medicare and Medicaid Investigations**: 
   - DOJ Civil Division CIDs regarding patient chart review processes and risk adjustment data submissions under Medicare Parts C and D
   - SDNY CID from May 2017 concerning alleged false claims for Medicare Part D prescription drug reimbursements

3. **Securities Class Action Lawsuits**: Multiple federal securities lawsuits filed beginning February 2019 against the company and officers/directors, alleging omissions and misrepresentations related to the LTC (Long-Term Care) business unit performance. Status includes one dismissal affirmed on appeal and others with pending motions to dismiss.

4. **ERISA Litigation**: Two consolidated class action lawsuits filed in 2020 alleging breach of fiduciary duties and prohibited transactions related to the Aetna 401(k) Plan's company stock investments, with the company obtaining a dismissal with prejudice in October 2022, though plaintiffs appealed.

5. **Delaware Shareholder Demand**: December 2021 demand for inspection of books and records related to potential fiduciary duty breaches concerning opioid-related matters.

6. **Other Legal Proceedings**: Various ongoing legal proceedings and government investigations involving claims related to bad faith, malpractice, claims processing, regulatory non-compliance, marketing misconduct, provider networks, and other contractual and employment matters.

nodes.title: Other Legal and Regulatory Proceedings
nodes.summary:
# Main Points Covered in the Partial Document

## Legal and Regulatory Matters

**Regulatory Audits and Compliance:**
- Premium and fee refunds or adjustments may result from regulatory audits (RADV, Public Exchange, CMS, OIG) and could materially adversely affect operating results
- RADV Audit Rule does not apply to CMS Part C Improper Payment Measures audits or HHS-RADV programs

**Government Investigations:**
- Company received Civil Investigative Demands (CIDs) from DOJ regarding patient chart review processes related to risk adjustment data submissions under Medicare Parts C and D
- Company received CID from SDNY in May 2017 concerning possible false claims for Medicare Part D prescription drug reimbursements
- Company is cooperating with government agencies in providing documents and information

## Stockholder and Securities Litigation

**Securities Class Actions:**
- Multiple class action complaints and derivative complaints filed beginning February 2019 against the Company and officers/directors regarding omissions and misrepresentations related to LTC business unit performance
- First-filed case dismissed with prejudice in February 2021; First Circuit affirmed dismissal in August 2022
- Other consolidated cases have amended complaints pending dismissal motions

**ERISA Class Actions:**
- Two ERISA class actions filed in August-September 2020 alleging breach of fiduciary duties related to Aetna 401(k) Plan investments
- District court granted Company's motion to dismiss with prejudice in October 2022; plaintiffs appealed to Second Circuit

**Delaware Shareholder Demand:**
- December 2021 demand for inspection of books and records related to potential fiduciary duty breaches concerning opioids

## Other Legal and Regulatory Proceedings

- Company involved in various legal proceedings including claims of bad faith, malpractice, breach of fiduciary duty, claims processing, medication dispensing, regulatory non-compliance, marketing misconduct, and other matters
- Government contract awards subject to protests by unsuccessful bidders
- Heightened regulatory review and litigation regarding business practices including premium rates, utilization management, medical policies, provider networks, pharmacy benefit management, and claim payment practices
- Potential consequences include restrictions on business, premium adjustments, refunds, fines, penalties, and possible suspension or loss of licensure

## Segment Reporting

- Company has three operating segments: Health Care Benefits, Pharmacy Services, and Retail/LTC, plus Corporate/Other
- Performance evaluated based on adjusted operating income (non-GAAP measure)
- Federal government revenues accounted for 18% of consolidated total revenues in 2022, primarily from CMS Medicare contracts

nodes.title: Segment Reporting
nodes.summary:
# Main Points Covered in the Partial Document

## Legal and Regulatory Matters
- The Company is defending against class action and derivative lawsuits
- Government contract awards (particularly Medicaid contracts) are subject to protests by unsuccessful bidders, which may result in reversals, delays, or modifications
- Heightened regulatory review and litigation regarding health care industry practices including premium rates, utilization management, medical policies, provider networks, pharmacy benefit management, and claim payment practices
- Government actions may prevent premium increases, restrict business operations, require business practice changes, result in refunds/payments, or lead to fines, penalties, or loss of licensure

## Segment Reporting and Financial Performance
- Company operates three main segments: Health Care Benefits, Pharmacy Services, and Retail/LTC, plus Corporate/Other
- Performance measured using adjusted operating income (non-GAAP measure)
- Federal government revenues represented 18% of consolidated total revenues in 2022
- Detailed financial data provided for 2022, 2021, and 2020 showing revenues, intersegment transactions, and adjusted operating income by segment

## Reconciliation of GAAP to Non-GAAP Measures
- Reconciliation of consolidated operating income to adjusted operating income
- Key adjustments include amortization of intangible assets, office real estate optimization charges, gains on divestitures, opioid litigation charges, and asset impairments
- Explanation of why certain items are excluded from adjusted operating income

## Auditor Reports and Internal Controls
- Ernst & Young LLP audit opinions on consolidated financial statements and internal control over financial reporting
- Management assessment concluding internal controls are effective as of December 31, 2022
- Critical audit matter identified: Valuation of health care costs payable (IBNR liabilities of $7.8 billion)
- No material changes in internal controls during the fourth quarter of 2022

nodes.title: Changes in and Disagreements With Accountants on Accounting and Financial Disclosure
nodes.summary:
# Description of Partial Document

This section of a Form 10-K filing covers three main regulatory compliance areas:

## Item 9: Accountant Changes and Disagreements
- States that no changes or disagreements with accountants regarding accounting and financial disclosure have occurred.

## Item 9A: Controls and Procedures
- **Disclosure Controls Evaluation**: The CEO and CFO confirm that disclosure controls and procedures were adequate and effective as of December 31, 2022, ensuring material information is communicated timely to officers.

- **Internal Control Over Financial Reporting**: Management outlines its responsibility for maintaining effective internal controls that ensure accurate financial record-keeping, proper authorization of transactions, and prevention of unauthorized asset use. The assessment was based on the COSO Internal Control-Integrated Framework (2013).

- **Control Assessment Results**: Management concluded that internal controls over financial reporting are effective as of December 31, 2022, providing reasonable assurance for asset safeguarding and reliable financial records.

- **Auditor Engagement**: Ernst & Young LLP was appointed to audit the consolidated financial statements and internal control over financial reporting in accordance with PCAOB standards.

- **Changes in Controls**: No material changes to internal control over financial reporting occurred during the fourth quarter of 2022.

## Item 9B: Other Information
- Confirms no reportable events occurred during the fourth quarter ended December 31, 2022.

# Description of Partial Document

This partial document from a Form 10-K filing covers several key governance and compensation topics for CVS Health Corporation:

## Main Points Covered:

1. **Accountant Changes (Item 9)**: Reports no changes in or disagreements with accountants regarding accounting and financial disclosure.

2. **Controls and Procedures (Item 9A)**:
   - CEO and CFO evaluation confirming disclosure controls and procedures were adequate and effective as of December 31, 2022
   - Management's responsibility for establishing and maintaining internal control over financial reporting
   - Assessment based on the Internal Control-Integrated Framework (2013)
   - Confirmation that internal control over financial reporting is effective with reasonable assurance
   - Ernst & Young LLP appointed as independent auditor
   - No material changes in internal control during Q4 2022

3. **Other Information (Item 9B)**: No reportable events occurred in Q4 2022.

4. **Foreign Jurisdiction Inspections (Item 9C)**: None reported.

5. **Directors and Executive Officers (Item 10)**: References to proxy statement sections covering board committees, code of conduct, and board nominee biographies.

6. **Executive Compensation (Item 11)**: References to proxy statement sections on director compensation and executive compensation details.

7. **Security Ownership and Equity Compensation Plans (Item 12)**:
   - Summary table of equity compensation plans showing 28,344 thousand securities available for issuance
   - Details on stockholder-approved and non-approved equity plans
   - Information on stock options, SARs, and restricted stock units as of December 31, 2022
   - Description of the Aetna Stock Plan and CVS Health 2017 Incentive Compensation Plan

# Description of Partial Document

This section of CVS Health Corporation's 10-K filing covers corporate governance and compensation matters:

## Main Points:

1. **Foreign Jurisdiction Inspections**: Confirms no disclosure issues regarding foreign jurisdictions preventing inspections.

2. **Directors and Corporate Governance (Item 10)**: References information about executive officers and incorporates by reference sections from the Proxy Statement covering board committees, code of conduct, audit committee reports, and board nominee biographies.

3. **Executive Compensation (Item 11)**: Incorporates by reference Proxy Statement sections detailing non-employee director compensation, executive compensation, compensation committee reports, and compensation discussion and analysis for named executive officers.

4. **Security Ownership and Equity Compensation Plans (Item 12)**: 
   - References Proxy Statement sections on share ownership by directors, executives, and principal stockholders
   - Presents a detailed table of equity compensation plan information as of December 31, 2022, showing:
     - 26.5 million securities issuable under stockholder-approved plans with weighted average exercise price of $75.70
     - 1.8 million securities under non-approved plans with exercise price of $43.60
     - 21.3 million securities remaining available for future issuance
   - Explains composition of securities (options, SARs, and restricted/deferred/performance stock units)
   - Notes the expired Aetna Stock Plan and the active CVS Health 2017 Incentive Compensation Plan

# Description of Partial Document

This partial document is from a 10-K filing for CVS Health Corporation and covers Items 13-15 of Part IV, focusing on corporate governance, accounting, and exhibits.

## Main Points Covered:

1. **Item 13 - Related Parties and Director Independence**: References sections in the Proxy Statement regarding director independence determinations and related person transaction policies.

2. **Item 14 - Principal Accountant Fees and Services**: Incorporates by reference the Proxy Statement section on ratification of the independent registered public accounting firm for 2022.

3. **Item 15 - Exhibits and Financial Statement Schedules**: Details the filing of financial statements, notes that financial statement schedules are omitted as not applicable, and provides an index of exhibits.

4. **Index to Exhibits**: Provides a comprehensive listing of exhibits filed or incorporated by reference, including:
   - Merger and acquisition agreements (Signify Health acquisition from September 2022)
   - Articles of incorporation and bylaws
   - Security instruments including indentures and various debt notes (2023-2048 notes)
   - References to management contracts and compensatory plans (marked with asterisks)

The document serves as a reference guide to corporate governance documents, financial statements, and legal instruments that define CVS Health's structure and obligations.

structure.summary:
# Description of Partial Document

This partial document is **Item 15 (Exhibits, Financial Statement Schedules) and Item 16 (Form 10-K Summary)** from CVS Health Corporation's 10-K filing for the fiscal year ended December 31, 2022.

## Main Points Covered:

1. **Financial Statements and Schedules**: References to consolidated financial statements located in Item 8, with a note that financial statement schedules are omitted as they are not applicable or required.

2. **Exhibits Index**: A comprehensive listing of exhibits filed or incorporated by reference, including:
   - **Acquisition Documents**: Merger agreement and voting agreement related to CVS Pharmacy's acquisition of Signify Health, Inc. (September 2022)
   - **Corporate Governance Documents**: Restated Certificate of Incorporation and Bylaws
   - **Debt Instruments**: Senior Indenture and various forms of notes with maturity dates ranging from 2023 to 2050
   - **Material Contracts**: Multiple credit agreements with Bank of America, N.A. as Administrative Agent
   - **Compensation and Incentive Plans**: Numerous employee benefit plans, stock option agreements, restricted stock unit agreements, performance-based compensation plans, and executive employment agreements
   - **Executive Agreements**: Change in control agreements, restrictive covenant agreements, and employment agreements for named executives (Larry Merlo, Eva Boratto, Jonathan Roberts, Thomas Moriarty, Karen S. Lynch, Alan Lotvin, and Shawn Guertin)
   - **Other Required Documents**: Subsidiary list, auditor consent, CEO and CFO certifications, and interactive XBRL data files

3. **Form 10-K Summary**: Noted as "None," indicating no summary was provided.

structure.title: Form 10-K Summary
structure.summary:
# Description of Partial Document

This partial document from CVS Health Corporation's Form 10-K filing contains the following main points:

1. **Exhibits List (Items 10.58-10.61)**: References to employee equity compensation agreements, including Restricted Stock Unit Agreements, Performance Stock Unit Agreements, and Nonqualified Stock Option Agreements for selected employees and executives, incorporated by reference from the Q1 2022 Form 10-Q.

2. **Corporate Documentation (Items 21-32)**: Lists of subsidiaries, expert consents (Ernst & Young LLP), and required certifications from the CEO and CFO under SEC rules 13a-14(a)/15d-14(a) and Section 1350.

3. **Interactive Data Files (Items 101-104)**: Inline XBRL formatted financial statements and data from the 2022 Annual Report, including consolidated statements of operations, comprehensive income, balance sheets, cash flows, shareholders' equity, and related notes.

4. **Signatures Section**: Official signatures from 13 company officers and directors dated February 8, 2023, including:
   - Karen S. Lynch (President and CEO)
   - Shawn M. Guertin (Executive Vice President and CFO)
   - James D. Clark (Senior Vice President and Controller)
   - Roger N. Farah (Chair of the Board)
   - Multiple board directors

The document serves as the closing section of CVS Health's Form 10-K annual report for fiscal year ended December 31, 2022.

structure.title: Signatures
structure.summary:
# Description of CVS Health Corporation Form 10-K Partial Document

This partial document from CVS Health Corporation's Annual Report (Form 10-K) for the year ended December 31, 2022, contains the following main sections:

## Signatures and Officer Certifications
- Executive signatures from key officers including CEO Karen S. Lynch and CFO Shawn M. Guertin, dated February 8, 2023
- Board member signatures from directors including Chair Roger N. Farah and other board members
- Certifications by the CEO and CFO regarding the accuracy of financial statements and effectiveness of internal controls (Exhibits 31.1, 31.2, 32.1, 32.2)

## Description of Common Stock (Exhibit 4.21)
- Summary of authorized capital stock: 3.2 billion shares of common stock, 120,619 shares of preferred stock, and 50 million shares of preference stock
- Voting rights: one vote per share with majority vote standard for uncontested director elections
- Dividend and liquidation rights of common stockholders
- Provisions regarding preemptive rights, charter amendments, and stockholder action by written consent
- Anti-takeover provisions including potential issuance of preferred stock and rights to purchase securities
- Delaware General Corporation Law Section 203 provisions regarding interested stockholders

## Subsidiaries List (Exhibit 21.1)
- Comprehensive listing of CVS Health Corporation's subsidiaries as of December 31, 2022
- Major subsidiaries include CVS Pharmacy, Inc., Aetna Inc., and numerous healthcare-related entities
- Subsidiaries organized by jurisdiction across the United States and internationally

## Auditor Consent (Exhibit 23.1)
- Consent from Ernst & Young LLP to incorporate audit reports by reference in registration statements
